schools.summary: The call to love and obey God — 'therefore thou shalt love the LORD thy God, and keep his charge... alway'; no break from the previous chapter — God had made Israel as the stars of heaven for multitude, building a small beginning into a great people, so 'thou shalt love the LORD,' the love the response of gratitude to so great a Benefactor (Ellicott, Henry); the whole address aiming that the people, allured by God's bounties, testify their gratitude by obedience and reverently embrace his law, Moses requiring love before and above all, love the spring of true obedience (Calvin); 'keep his keeping,' observe all that God gave to be observed, the whole law in all its parts; 'alway' — not by fits but continually; love coming first and obedience flowing from it, the keeping of the commandments the fruit and proof of love (John 14:15).

themes: thou shalt love the LORD thy God
keep his charge and commandments
alway
gratitude issues in love and obedience

schools.summary: The present generation as eyewitnesses of God's acts — 'know ye this day: for I speak not with your children which have not known... the chastisement of the LORD your God, his greatness, his mighty hand'; 'know' not a bare knowing but acknowledging and considering with diligence and thankfulness, laying it to heart (Wesley); Moses addressing those who had themselves seen God's works, for all under twenty at the Exodus would still be living and must remember the events of their youth, the appeal to eyewitnesses not a later generation who knew the acts by report (Ellicott); what they had witnessed — both the chastisement God laid on his people and the mighty wonders, his power and his discipline alike; the remembrance to awaken and strengthen their love to God (Keil and Delitzsch); the people more than sufficiently taught by God's illustrious acts how great his power and remarkable his mercy (Calvin); experience of God laying an obligation of love and obedience.

themes: know ye this day
not your children, but you
the chastisement and might of God
eyewitnesses bound to love him

schools.summary: The wonders done in Egypt — 'his miracles, and his acts, which he did in the midst of Egypt unto Pharaoh the king of Egypt, and unto all his land'; the miraculous works done in Egypt, the ten plagues inflicted on the Egyptians for refusing to let Israel go, the mighty signs by which God broke the power of Egypt and brought his people out (Gill); the judgments falling on the king who had oppressed Israel and on his whole land, God dealing with the proudest of kings and the mightiest of nations and overcoming them; the people having themselves seen these things — the river turned to blood, the darkness, the death of the firstborn — the memory binding their hearts to the God who delivered them; the rehearsing of God's acts a chief means to stir up love and obedience; the wonders of the Exodus the foundation of Israel's faith.

themes: his miracles and his acts
in the midst of Egypt
unto Pharaoh and all his land
the wonders of the Exodus

schools.summary: The overthrow at the Red Sea — 'how he made the water of the Red sea to overflow them... and how the LORD hath destroyed them unto this day'; the numerous army of Egypt, with horses and chariots, pursuing Israel to the sea to bring them back or destroy them, and God making the waters return upon them and drown them, the host that hunted Israel perishing in the deep (Ex 14:28, Gill); 'unto this day' — the effect of that destruction continuing in the lasting weakness and fear of Egypt and the safety of Israel from any further attempt, the blow breaking Egypt's power for good (Wesley); the people having seen with their own eyes the proud army of their oppressors swallowed by the sea while they passed through on dry ground, no deliverance more signal; the God who claimed their love having utterly destroyed their pursuing host, his salvation complete; the destruction of the enemy the salvation of the people.

themes: the army of Egypt, horses and chariots
the Red sea to overflow them
destroyed them unto this day
the great deliverance remembered

schools.summary: God's dealings in the wilderness — 'what he did unto you in the wilderness, until ye came unto this place'; the whole long history of God's working with them through the forty years, from the Red Sea to the plains of Moab; chiefly meant not so much the good things — manna, water, guidance — as the chastisements and corrections God exercised them with for their murmurings, rebellions, and unbelief, the wilderness a place of discipline as well as provision (Gill); Moses setting before the people in brief summary the acts of God, to heighten the authority of the Law and bind them to obedience (Calvin); God's chastisements, no less than his mercies, tokens of his care, meant to humble, prove, and correct, both blessing and discipline teaching them to fear and love him; God dealing with them as a father with his children (Deut 8:5).

themes: what he did unto you in the wilderness
until ye came unto this place
chastisement as well as care
God's corrections part of his love

schools.summary: The judgment on Dathan and Abiram — 'the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed them up... in the midst of all Israel'; these Reubenites, with Korah, having risen against Moses and Aaron, quarrelling against God's appointed authority, the earth opening and swallowing them alive with all that pertained to them (Num 16:31, Gill); done openly, before the eyes of the whole congregation, that all might see and fear; the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram impossible to separate from that of Korah, whose whole quarrel was over the priesthood, though here only the Reubenites are named (swallowed by the earth, while Korah's company perished by fire), the rebellion one (Ellicott); the people having seen how God punishes those who rise against his ordinance, the memory keeping them from the like rebellion; to resist God's appointed order being to court his judgment.

themes: Dathan and Abiram
the earth opened and swallowed them
in the midst of all Israel
judgment on rebellion

schools.summary: The people as eyewitnesses — 'your eyes have seen all the great acts of the LORD which he did'; literally 'your eyes are the witnesses, the seers, of all the great working of Jehovah' (Ellicott); the people not hearers only but eyewitnesses of God's mighty deeds, unable to plead ignorance, for they had seen; all of them having seen some, and some of them all, of the great things in Egypt, at the Red Sea, and in the wilderness, the whole generation a body of witnesses (Wesley); what a man has seen with his own eyes binding him more than what he has only heard, the people who beheld God's acts the more bound to love and obey, the less excusable if they rebelled; what our eyes have seen, especially in early days, to be improved by us, the mercies and judgments witnessed in youth laid to heart as the ground of lifelong obedience (Wesley).

themes: your eyes have seen
all the great acts of the LORD
eyewitnesses, not hearers only
seen mercies bind the deepest

schools.summary: Obedience the way to strength and possession — 'therefore shall ye keep all the commandments... that ye may be strong, and go in and possess the land'; because God can execute such terrible judgments (as on Dathan and Abiram) and has given such proofs of his power, the people should keep his commandments lest they provoke the like (Clarke); obedience making them strong for the conquest and bringing them into the inheritance, 'go in and possess' meaning to complete the conquest in detail so as to enjoy the whole profit of the land (Ellicott); 'the commandment' in the singular, marking one settled course of obedient action rather than a heap of details, the law one way to be walked in; Moses, loth to conclude, still urging that keeping the commandments is the way to enter and enjoy the good land, a type of the life to come — if thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments (Henry); strength for duty and enjoyment of God's gifts going with obedience.

themes: keep all the commandments
that ye may be strong
go in and possess the land
obedience the way to the inheritance

schools.summary: The promise of long days in the good land — 'that ye may prolong your days in the land, which the LORD sware unto your fathers... a land that floweth with milk and honey'; obedience securing not merely the taking of the land but the long continuance of the people in it, their abiding in the inheritance depending on keeping the law (Gill); 'a land that floweth with milk and honey,' the well-known token of its richness and plenty, a land of abundance and delight, God giving no barren or grudging country but a good and fruitful one; the land theirs by God's oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, a sure inheritance founded on the divine promise; obedience the way not only to obtain God's blessings but to keep and enjoy them long, the people holding the land as long as they held to God and losing it when they forsook him.

themes: prolong your days in the land
which the LORD sware unto your fathers
a land flowing with milk and honey
enjoyment bound to obedience

schools.summary: The contrast with Egypt's watering by labour — 'not as the land of Egypt... where thou sowedst thy seed, and wateredst it with thy foot, as a garden of herbs'; in Egypt rain scarcely ever falling, God supplying its lack by the yearly inundation of the Nile, and where the flood does not reach, water collected in ponds and channels and brought by labour (Clarke); 'watering with the foot' done with great pains and toil — fetching and dispersing water, digging furrows, working the engines and treadwheels that raised the Nile's water, the husbandman watering his ground as a garden of herbs by ceaseless labour (Wesley); the land of Canaan not like Egypt, dependent on the river and man's toil, but drinking the rain of heaven, watered directly by God's hand; though Egypt was praised above all lands for fertility (Gen 13:10), Canaan better still, depending not on the river but on God (Ellicott); Egypt's watering by labour and the eye to the river, Canaan's by faith and the eye to heaven.

themes: not as the land of Egypt
wateredst it with thy foot
Egypt's toil by the river
dependence taught by contrast

schools.summary: Canaan, the land that drinks rain from heaven — 'a land of hills and valleys, and drinketh water of the rain of heaven'; being hilly and uneven, it could not be watered as Egypt by the overflow of a river and the cutting of canals across a flat plain (Gill), and was thereby more healthful than Egypt, which the Nile's yearly overflow rendered unpleasant (Wesley); watered not by the labour of men nor by a river but directly by the rain God sent from above, 'while thou sleepest on thy bed, the Holy One waters it high and low' (the Jewish commentator), like the parable of the seed growing the husbandman knows not how (Mark 4:26-27, Ellicott); Canaan kept in immediate dependence on God, whereas Egypt looked to its river and its own toil, Israel must look up to heaven for the rain, their harvests hanging on God's blessing not their engines; the land that drinks the rain of heaven the emblem of a people who live by faith on God.

themes: a land of hills and valleys
drinketh the rain of heaven
God himself waters it
dependence on God for daily bread

schools.summary: God's constant care over the land — 'a land which the LORD thy God careth for: the eyes of the LORD thy God are always upon it, from the beginning of the year even unto the end of the year'; the Hebrew 'seeketh' or 'searcheth out,' as of a land God had espied and chosen, the glory of all lands (Ezek 20:6, Ellicott); while God has a general providence over the whole earth, he cares for this land in a special and peculiar manner (Gill); God's watchful eye resting on the land continually, all the year round, ordering its seasons, rains, and fruitfulness, his care never sleeping; God watering it as by his own hand without man's help, giving peculiar blessings Egypt enjoyed not (Wesley); the land's fruitfulness hanging on God's care, his people ever in his sight and under his hand; the typology — as God's eyes always on the land, so his eye ever on his church and people (1 Pet 3:12).

themes: a land which the LORD careth for
his eyes always upon it
from the beginning to the end of the year
under God's constant care

schools.summary: The condition of love and whole-hearted service — 'if ye shall hearken diligently unto my commandments... to love the LORD your God, and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul'; the blessing to follow conditioned on a diligent, hearty obedience springing from love, not a cold or partial keeping but the service of the whole soul; here beginning the formal sanction of the charge, the declaration of rewards and punishments, a characteristic feature of the Law, the covenant setting blessing and curse before the people (Ellicott); the commandments given in the name and by the authority of the Lord, the only lawgiver, the obedience required to God himself not to man (Gill); 'with all your heart and with all your soul' — the whole inner man, the affections and will, engaged, God served with the heart and that wholly; the love God requires the spring of the obedience he rewards, as the gospel too binds love and obedience (Mt 22:37).

themes: if ye hearken diligently
to love the LORD your God
serve him with all your heart and soul
whole-hearted love the condition

schools.summary: The promise of seasonable rain — 'I will give you the rain of your land in his due season, the first rain and the latter rain, that thou mayest gather in thy corn, and thy wine, and thine oil'; God giving a sufficient quantity of rain at the proper times to make the land fruitful, the very rain on which the land depended God himself sending to the obedient (Gill, Keil); the two rains — the former or early rain, falling about November when the seed was sown, to prepare the ground, and the latter or spring rain, just before harvest, to swell and ripen the grain, both needed and both promised in their season (Joel 2:23, James 5:7, Clarke, Ellicott); the three staples — corn, wine, and oil — brought in abundantly when God gave the rain; the seasonable rain God's gift to the obedient, their harvests the reward of faithfulness; God tying the blessings of nature to the obedience of his people, godliness having the promise of the life that now is (1 Tim 4:8).

themes: the rain of your land in his due season
the first rain and the latter rain
gather in corn, wine, and oil
harvests reward obedience

schools.summary: The promise of plenty and fulness — 'I will send grass in thy fields for thy cattle, that thou mayest eat and be full'; by the plentiful showers given at proper times God causing the grass to spring up, food for the cattle of every sort, the rain that fed the corn feeding also the herds (Gill); 'that thou mayest eat and be full' — a further mercy belonging not only to the abundance of food but to the food itself in man's inward parts, for it is possible to eat and not be satisfied, but God promising both plenty and the satisfying of it (Ellicott); godliness having the promise of the life that now is, yet the favour of God putting more gladness into the heart than the increase of corn, wine, and oil (Ps 4:7), the best of the blessing being God himself (Wesley); God giving his obedient people not bare provision but plenty, and contentment in it, satisfying the desire of every living thing.

themes: grass in thy fields for thy cattle
that thou mayest eat and be full
plenty and satisfaction
God's favour the best blessing

schools.summary: The warning against the deceived heart and idolatry — 'take heed to yourselves, that your heart be not deceived, and ye turn aside, and serve other gods'; the warning bearing especially on the time when they are full and satisfied, for prosperity is a snare and the well-fed heart apt to forget God (Deut 8:10, Ellicott); the particular danger — the heart deceived by observing the influence of the heavens (sun, moon, stars) on the earth's fruitfulness, drawn to worship the host of heaven, ascribing to the creature the gifts of the Creator (Gill); Moses, by often inculcating the caution, arraigning man's proneness to superstition, 'that your heart be not deceived' owning how easily men are deluded (Calvin); the progress of the sin — first the heart deceived, then the feet turn aside, then they serve other gods, the apostasy beginning within; the great peril of a prosperous people to forget the God who gives the plenty.

themes: take heed to yourselves
that your heart be not deceived
turn aside and serve other gods
plenty must not breed apostasy

schools.summary: The judgment of drought — 'then the LORD's wrath be kindled against you, and he shut up the heaven, that there be no rain... and lest ye perish quickly from off the good land'; nothing more provoking to God than idolatry, contrary to his very nature and to his honour and glory, the turning to other gods rousing his anger as nothing else (Gill); the very blessing promised to obedience, the rain of heaven, withheld from the rebellious, heaven as a great storehouse of rain (Job 38:22) whose doors God opens to give and shuts to withhold, the same hand that watered the land making it barren in wrath (Wesley); the land that depended wholly on the rain soon failing without it, and the people with it, their hold on the good land as quickly lost as given if they forsook God; the gifts of God held on the condition of obedience, the same providence that blesses the faithful judging the rebellious; God's gifts withdrawn his sorest judgments.

themes: the LORD's wrath be kindled
he shut up the heaven, no rain
perish quickly from the good land
the same rain blesses or judges

schools.summary: The laying up of God's words in the heart — 'lay up these my words in your heart and in your soul, and bind them for a sign upon your hand, that they may be as frontlets between your eyes'; to lay up in or on the heart being to hide deeply in it, that the word not be lightly received and soon let slip but treasured in the inmost soul, the heart filled with the word as a storehouse to be used on all occasions (Calvin, Wesley); the outward signs the same injunctions as Deut 6:6-9, the word bound on the hand to guide the doing and set between the eyes to guide the thinking, governing the whole life — the three rules, the heart filled, the eyes fixed, the hands employed (Clarke, Ellicott, Wesley); Moses repeating the directions for keeping up religion, knowing how soon the word is forgotten (Henry); the surest guard against the deceived heart and apostasy the word of God hidden deep in the soul (Ps 119:11).

themes: lay up these my words in your heart
bind them for a sign upon your hand
frontlets between your eyes
the word hidden guards the heart

schools.summary: The promise of long days as the days of heaven — 'that your days may be multiplied, and the days of your children... as the days of heaven upon the earth'; long life a desirable blessing promised to those who keep the law, extended to their children after them, the obedience of the fathers blessing the generations (Gill); 'as the days of heaven upon the earth' meaning as long as the heaven keeps its place and continues its influence, a very long and enduring time (Wesley); the verse saying the land was sworn 'to give them' (the fathers) not 'to give you,' from which the Rabbis argued that the resurrection of the dead is taught in the Law, since the fathers must rise to possess it — a remark worthy of a Christian commentator, near our Lord's own argument (Mt 22:31-32, Ellicott); obedience securing not only the present generation but their seed, the promise of long days pointing beyond the land to the everlasting life of God's people.

themes: that your days may be multiplied
and the days of your children
as the days of heaven upon the earth
the promise points beyond this life

schools.summary: The call to love, walk in God's ways, and cleave to him — 'to love the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways, and to cleave unto him'; love the spring, walking in his ways the course, cleaving the steadfast adherence, the whole man given to God in heart, life, and unwavering attachment; the keeping required not a bare notional knowledge but the putting into practice, true keeping being doing (Gill); 'walking in all his ways' meaning to imitate God — 'he is compassionate, and thou shalt be compassionate; he showeth mercies, and thou shalt show mercies' (the Jewish comment, worthy of the NT, near our Lord's 'be ye merciful, as your Father is merciful,' Luke 6:36, Ellicott); what follows showing the need of a mediator, for such love and likeness to God are beyond man's natural power; 'cleave unto him' — to hold fast to God in unbroken loyalty, not drawn away by any other.

themes: to love the LORD your God
walk in all his ways
cleave unto him
walking in his ways is imitating him

schools.summary: The promise to drive out the nations — 'then will the LORD drive out all these nations from before you, and ye shall possess greater nations and mightier than yourselves'; God driving out all the seven nations that then inhabited the land, by little and little, making room for Israel to inherit, the conquest God's doing, the obedient people the instruments of his hand (Gill); the nations to be dispossessed larger and stronger than Israel, the victory not by Israel's own might, for they were the weaker, but by the power of God who fought for them; the conquest hanging on the people's love and obedience (v22), if they cleaved to God he would overthrow nations far stronger than they, their strength not in themselves but in their God; no enemy, however great, able to stand before a people whom God favoured; the battle the LORD's.

themes: the LORD drive out all these nations
greater and mightier than yourselves
won by God's hand
the battle is the LORD's

schools.summary: The extent of the promised land — 'every place whereon the soles of your feet shall tread shall be yours: from the wilderness and Lebanon, from the river Euphrates, even unto the uttermost sea'; the bounds from the river Euphrates on the east to the uttermost (Mediterranean) sea on the west, from the southern wilderness to Lebanon in the north, the full compass promised to Abraham (Gen 15:18, Clarke); the promise, notwithstanding Israel's provocations, made good in the time of Solomon, whose dominion reached from the Euphrates to the border of Egypt (1 Kgs 4:21); 'every place the foot treads' not absolute, as the Rabbins fondly conceived, but restrained to the promised land and held on condition of obedience (Gill, Wesley); the land theirs as they went in and possessed it, foot by foot, by faith and obedience; God's gift large and his promise sure, yet entered by an obedient people.

themes: every place the soles of your feet tread
from the Euphrates to the uttermost sea
fulfilled in Solomon's time
held on condition of obedience

schools.summary: The fear of Israel upon the nations — 'there shall no man be able to stand before you: for the LORD your God shall lay the fear of you and the dread of you upon all the land that ye shall tread upon'; not a single man, however mighty (no Og, no son of Anak), nor any nation able to withstand Israel, the obedient people invincible not by their own strength but by God's (Gill); God going before his people, striking terror into the hearts of their enemies, so the nations unmanned and falling before them, the battle half won by the fear God sent ahead; Rashi's distinction, 'the fear upon those near, the dread upon those far off,' the promise far-reaching, 'upon all the land that ye shall tread' (Ellicott); when God is with his people no enemy can stand, he fighting for them by the terror he casts on their foes, the same fear that fell on Jericho (Josh 2:9); God's presence making his people dreadful to their enemies.

themes: no man able to stand before you
the fear and dread of you
upon all the land ye tread
God's presence makes them dreadful

schools.summary: The blessing and the curse set before the people — 'Behold, I set before you this day a blessing and a curse'; Moses concluding his exhortation in a most affecting way, laying the whole issue of life and death before them (Henry); the simple terms — blessing and cursing, the blessing if they obey, the curse if they do not, the alternative plain and the choice momentous (Ellicott); God dealing with men as responsible beings, setting good and evil before them and pressing them to choose the good (Deut 30:19); the blessing and curse real, and the choosing theirs.

Clarke: if God had not put it in the people's power either to obey or disobey, if they had not had a free will over which they had authority, the setting of blessing and curse before them would be absurd and meaningless; the very offer supposes their responsibility and power to choose.

Calvin: Moses embraces the two at once — the people would be blessed if they earnestly applied themselves to keeping the Law, and cursed if they shook off its yoke and revelled in their lusts; the covenant holding out both, that the people might be moved by hope and warned by fear.

debates.question: Does setting 'a blessing and a curse' before the people imply free will, or is it consistent with God's sovereign grace?

sides.position: it implies free will — if God had not put it in their power to obey or disobey, the setting of blessing and curse before them would be absurd; the offer supposes their responsibility and power to choose

it sets out, in covenant fashion, blessing for those who keep the Law and curse for those who shake off its yoke — holding out both that the people might be moved by hope and warned by fear, consistent with God's sovereign ordering

themes: I set before you a blessing and a curse
life and death set before them
the choice is theirs
moved by hope, warned by fear

schools.summary: The blessing promised to obedience — 'a blessing, if ye obey the commandments of the LORD your God, which I command you this day'; the blessing not unconditional but hanging upon the people's obeying the commandments God gave them, to obey being to be blessed; all temporal blessings the people stood in need of coming upon the obedient — blessed in body and estate, in their families, flocks and herds, in city and field, going out and coming in, the whole of life under God's favour (as the fuller list of Deut 28:3 sets out, Gill); God tying his blessing to obedience not arbitrarily but as the fit and ordered fruit of a right relation to him, the obedient walking in the way of blessing; God ready and willing to bless his people, asking only that they obey; obedience and blessing joined in God's ordering, the way of God's commandments the way of true and lasting good.

themes: a blessing, if ye obey
the commandments of the LORD
obedience the way of blessing
God ready to bless

schools.summary: The curse upon disobedience and apostasy — 'a curse, if ye will not obey the commandments... but turn aside out of the way... to go after other gods, which ye have not known'; as the blessing came upon obedience, so the curse upon refusing to obey, the disobedient accursed in body and estate, family, flock, and herd, in city and field (as Deut 28:16 sets out, Gill); the curse bound especially to apostasy, the leaving of God's way to follow idols, the deepest disobedience the forsaking of God for false gods; 'which ye have not known' — other gods the people had no acquaintance with, nor any experience of their power, wisdom, or goodness, as they had of the LORD's, the folly of apostasy being to forsake the God they have proved for gods they have never known to do them good (Wesley); to turn from the known and tried God to unknown idols both sin and madness; God setting the curse before them not to destroy but to warn and deter.

themes: a curse, if ye will not obey
turn aside to go after other gods
which ye have not known
apostasy brings the curse

schools.summary: The blessing and curse set on the two mountains — 'thou shalt put the blessing upon mount Gerizim, and the curse upon mount Ebal'; 'put' meaning 'speak or pronounce, or cause to be pronounced' (Wesley), the blessings to be solemnly proclaimed from or toward Gerizim, the curses from or toward Ebal, in a great public ceremony (set out in Deut 27:12, fulfilled in Josh 8:33); the Targum reading 'those that bless' and 'those that curse,' the tribes divided half on each mount to answer Amen (Ellicott); the names' etymology casting light, Gerizim from a root meaning 'to cut' (Clarke), the two mountains over against each other with the valley of Shechem between making a natural amphitheatre; the covenant not only spoken by Moses but ratified by the whole nation in the land, the people owning the terms with their Amen, the blessing and curse set before their eyes and sealed by their own mouths; God appointing the solemn rite to impress the covenant deeply (Calvin).

themes: the blessing upon mount Gerizim
the curse upon mount Ebal
the covenant solemnly proclaimed
sealed by the people's Amen

schools.summary: The location of the mountains by the oaks of Moreh — 'on the other side Jordan... in the champaign over against Gilgal, beside the plains of Moreh'; the mountains lying across the Jordan from where Moses stood in the plains of Moab, in Canaan proper, near Shechem; the form of the question 'are they not...?' affirming the more vehemently that these mountains were indeed in the land they were to possess, Moses appealing to the people's knowledge to confirm the certainty of their entering (Calvin); 'where the sun goeth down' a memorable attestation of the speaker's position east of Jordan, travellers having seen the sun set in the very gap between the two mountains (Ellicott); 'the plains (or oaks) of Moreh' one of the first places Abram came to in Canaan, where God appeared and promised the land to his seed (Gen 12:6-7, Wesley), so the covenant ratified at the place where it was first promised, the seed entering on the inheritance pledged to Abraham.

themes: on the other side Jordan
beside the plains of Moreh
where the sun goeth down
ratified where first promised to Abram

schools.summary: The assurance of passing over to possess the land — 'ye shall pass over Jordan to go in to possess the land which the LORD your God giveth you, and ye shall possess it, and dwell therein'; the people now near the land, assured that they should certainly cross the river, take possession, and settle in the inheritance God gave them, the entry sure, founded on God's gift (Gill); the place named (Shechem, by the oak of Moreh) where the LORD first appeared to Abram and said 'Unto thy seed will I give this land' (Gen 12:7), the first recorded promise of the land to the patriarch, so the people coming to where the promise had first been spoken to their father, the seed entering on what was pledged to Abraham (Ellicott); what God promised to Abraham and now renewed to his seed surely performed, the possession as sure as God's word given to the obedient; the gift of the land and the duty of obedience going together.

themes: ye shall pass over Jordan
possess the land, and dwell therein
where the promise was first given to Abram
the possession sure as God's word

schools.summary: The closing charge to do all the statutes — 'ye shall observe to do all the statutes and judgments which I set before you this day'; the people to take notice of the laws and give heed to them, not for knowledge only but so as to practise them, the observing to issue in doing (Gill); 'all the statutes and judgments' — not a chosen part but the whole body of God's laws, the obedience to be entire; having assured them of entering and possessing the land (v31), Moses pressing that their continuance and welfare in it depended on keeping the law, the gift God's but the holding of it by obedience; the whole discourse — the rehearsal of God's acts, the promise of plenty, the warning of curse, the setting of blessing and curse — coming to rest in this one charge to observe to do all God commands; the hearing of God's word to issue in the doing, the doer not the hearer only blessed (Jas 1:22); the chapter ending where it began, in the call to love and obey God.

themes: observe to do all the statutes
which I set before you this day
possession held by obedience
hearing issues in doing
